states.name: New York
states.framework: NYDFS BitLicense / Limited Purpose Trust Charter
states.detail: Most coins commonly called 'stablecoins' count as Virtual Currency under New York's rules, which means issuing one requires a BitLicense or a New York-chartered trust company (the trust route also lets a company handle money transmission and fiduciary duties without extra licenses, which a BitLicense alone doesn't allow). Before issuing, a coin also needs DFS sign-off through self-certification, a spot on DFS's approved 'Greenlist,' or individual approval. Coins already on the Greenlist include Gemini Dollar, Ripple USD, and WisdomTree Dollar.
states.effective: In force since Jun. 8, 2022 guidance
states.sourceText: NY Dept. of Financial Services, Virtual Currency Businesses

states.name: California
states.framework: Digital Financial Assets Law (DFAL)
states.detail: California's Digital Financial Assets Law entered into force on July 1, 2026. Any exchange, custodian, stablecoin issuer, or Bitcoin ATM operator serving California residents must now hold a DFPI license or have filed a complete application, or face civil enforcement of up to $100,000 per day. The law bars licensees from exchanging a stablecoin unless its issuer is licensed, has applied for a license, or is a qualified bank or trust company backing the coin with eligible securities. DFPI expects an initial $100,000 net worth, subject to change, and began accepting applications through NMLS on March 9, 2026.
states.effective: In force since Jul. 1, 2026; DFPI license or complete application now required; enforcement active
states.sourceText: AB 39, Financial Code Div. 1.25

states.name: Texas
states.framework: Money Services Modernization Act (Finance Code Ch. 152)
states.detail: Texas treats qualifying stablecoins as 'money,' which means issuing or transmitting them requires a money transmission license. To count, a stablecoin must be pegged to a currency like the dollar, fully backed by reserve assets, and redeemable for that currency on demand. Licensees must hold a minimum net worth that scales with their size, starting at $100,000. Texas also lets a licensee hold stablecoin itself as part of its required reserves, as long as it's the same stablecoin it owes to customers and is kept in proper custody.
states.effective: In force since Sept. 1, 2023
states.sourceText: Tex. Finance Code Ch. 152, enacted by S.B. 895 (2023)

states.name: Florida
states.framework: Payment Stablecoin Licensing (HB 175) + State Pilot (SB 1568)
states.detail: Florida enacted a package of digital-asset laws signed by Governor DeSantis in late June 2026, all effective immediately. HB 175 requires a money services business to hold a license from the Office of Financial Regulation before issuing a payment stablecoin, with reserves backed one-to-one by cash or similarly safe assets and verified monthly by a CPA, aligning the state framework with the federal GENIUS Act. SB 1568 establishes the Florida Stablecoin Pilot Program, allowing the Department of Financial Services to accept approved payment stablecoins for certain government fees. A companion law, HB 505, requires virtual-currency kiosk operators to register with the OFR.
states.effective: HB 175 and SB 1568 signed by Gov. DeSantis, late Jun. 2026; effective immediately
states.sourceText: Florida House/Senate, HB 175 and SB 1568 bill history

states.name: Wyoming
states.framework: Wyoming Stable Token Act
states.detail: The only state to both regulate and directly issue a stablecoin. The Wyoming Stable Token Commission's Frontier Stable Token (FRNT) can only be backed by cash, US Treasuries maturing within a year, or short-term Treasury repurchase agreements, per W.S. 40-31-106(a). A Trust Account must hold 100% of FRNT's notional value, plus a separate Liquidity Fund Account targeting another 2% as a buffer, with leftover investment earnings flowing quarterly to a state school foundation program. FRNT runs across eight blockchains and went live for public purchase on January 7, 2026, redeemable for $1.00.
states.effective: Mainnet live since Aug. 18, 2025; public purchase since Jan. 7, 2026
states.sourceText: Wyoming Stable Token Commission Factbook

states.name: Illinois
states.framework: Digital Assets and Consumer Protection Act (DACPA) + Digital Asset Kiosk Act (DAKA)
states.detail: Signed the same day, August 18, 2025 (DACPA enacted as Public Act 104-0428), these twin laws split digital asset oversight in two. DACPA defines a 'digital asset' broadly enough to deliberately capture stablecoins: an asset normally excluded for not being used as an investment loses that exclusion if it's marketed as holding a stable, effectively fixed value, which is exactly how a stablecoin is sold. Businesses must register with IDFPR by July 1, 2027, though customer disclosure and custody protections apply sooner. DAKA separately regulates digital asset kiosks specifically, capping daily transactions at $2,500 for new customers and $10,500 for existing ones, and limiting fees to the greater of $5 or 18% of the transaction.
states.effective: Both effective Aug. 18, 2025; DACPA registration deadline Jul. 1, 2027
states.sourceText: 205 ILCS 731/ and 205 ILCS 732/, Illinois Compiled Statutes

states.name: Colorado
states.framework: Model Money Transmission Modernization Act (HB 25-1201)
states.detail: Colorado repealed its old Money Transmitters Act and replaced it with the CSBS Model Money Transmission Modernization Act, enabling multistate licensing participation. Unlike Texas, Colorado writes no virtual currency or stablecoin language into the statute at all: 'monetary value' is defined simply as 'a medium of exchange, whether or not redeemable in money,' leaving it to interpretation whether a given stablecoin activity falls within that generic definition. Its net worth formula is identical to Texas's, since both states adopted the same model law: $100,000 or 3% of assets up to $100 million, then a declining percentage above that.
states.effective: Signed Apr. 18, 2025; effective Aug. 6, 2025
states.sourceText: Colorado General Assembly, HB 25-1201

states.name: Washington
states.framework: Uniform Money Services Act
states.detail: Washington's statute defines 'virtual currency' directly and writes it into the definition of 'money transmission' itself, so there's no separate crypto license, just the existing money transmitter framework. Virtual currency licensees face specific disclosure duties: fee schedules, whether funds are insured, a notice that virtual currency transfers are irrevocable, and how errors get resolved. The standard net worth formula (as low as $10,000) jumps to a $100,000 minimum specifically for companies that provide virtual currency storage, though that figure comes from the implementing regulation, not the statute itself.
states.effective: In force

states.name: Arizona
states.framework: CSBS Money Transmission Modernization Act (SB 1580, 2022)
states.detail: Arizona replaced its old money transmitter statute with the CSBS model act in 2022 (SB 1580, Chapter 236). Like Colorado, the new statute defines 'monetary value' as simply 'a medium of exchange, whether or not redeemable in money,' with no explicit virtual currency or stablecoin definition. Whether a given stablecoin activity triggers licensing depends on how Arizona DIFI interprets that generic definition. Cryptocurrency kiosk operators face separate, explicit requirements under A.R.S. 6-1236, including daily transaction caps of $2,000 for new customers and $10,500 for existing ones, mandatory fraud warnings, and blockchain analytics requirements. Net worth follows the same formula as Colorado and Texas: $100,000 or 3% of total assets up to $100 million.
states.effective: Signed May 20, 2022
states.sourceText: A.R.S. Title 6, Chapter 12 (SB 1580, Ch. 236, 2022)

states.name: Georgia
states.framework: Georgia Payment Stablecoin Act (HB 1272)
states.detail: Georgia enacted the Georgia Payment Stablecoin Act (HB 1272) in 2026, a dedicated licensing regime for payment stablecoin issuers, but it is not yet in force. Under Section 4, and subject to appropriations by the General Assembly, it becomes effective on the earlier of January 18, 2027 or 120 days after the issuance of final federal GENIUS Act implementing regulations. The Act carves payment stablecoin issuance out of Georgia's existing money transmission laws and authorizes the Department of Banking and Finance to license issuers, on a framework the statute requires to be substantially similar to the GENIUS Act. Licensed issuers must back outstanding stablecoins one-to-one with liquid reserves held in trust for consumers and maintain redemption policies. Beginning July 18, 2028, it becomes unlawful to offer or sell a payment stablecoin in Georgia unless the issuer is a permitted, licensed, or federally qualified issuer. Separately, some virtual-currency money transmission already requires a license under O.C.G.A. Sec. 7-1-680 et seq.
states.effective: Enacted 2026; effective (subject to state appropriations) the earlier of Jan. 18, 2027 or 120 days after federal GENIUS Act final rules; issuer requirement from Jul. 18, 2028
states.sourceText: HB 1272, Georgia Payment Stablecoin Act (as passed House and Senate)

states.name: North Carolina
states.framework: Money Transmitters Act (N.C. Gen. Stat. Ch. 53, Art. 16B)
states.detail: North Carolina regulates stablecoin and virtual-currency activity through its Money Transmitters Act (Chapter 53, Article 16B), administered by the Office of the Commissioner of Banks (NCCOB). The statute expressly defines virtual currency and brings money transmission conducted in whole or in part in virtual currency, including maintaining control of virtual currency on behalf of others, within its licensing requirement. A person engaging in money transmission must obtain a license through NMLS; the license is perpetual but requires an annual NMLS renewal filing. North Carolina does not yet have a dedicated stablecoin-issuer statute. A separate bill, the Virtual Currency Kiosk Consumer Protection Act (HB 920), would place crypto-ATM operators under NCCOB supervision, with implementing rules due by December 1, 2026.
states.effective: Money Transmitters Act in force; virtual currency covered; kiosk bill (HB 920) pending, rules due Dec. 1, 2026
states.sourceText: N.C. Gen. Stat. Ch. 53, Art. 16B (Money Transmitters Act); NC Office of the Commissioner of Banks

states.name: Connecticut
states.framework: Money Transmission Act (virtual currency revisions, PA 25-66 / HB 7082)
states.detail: Connecticut revised its money transmission law to regulate virtual currency, effective October 1, 2025, administered by the Department of Banking. A person engaged in virtual currency business in Connecticut, by receiving, transmitting, storing, or maintaining custody or control of virtual currency, must hold virtual currency of the same type and amount owed or obligated to each customer. Licensees may not lend, hypothecate, pledge, or otherwise use or encumber customer virtual currency without authorization, and the act extends risk-disclosure and receipt requirements to virtual currency transmitters and kiosk operators. Custody may be delegated only to parties qualified to hold it.
states.effective: In force since Oct. 1, 2025
states.sourceText: Conn. Public Act 25-66 (HB 7082); CT Dept. of Banking

states.name: Nevada
states.framework: Money transmission law (NRS Ch. 671), applied to digital currency by FID guidance
states.detail: Nevada has no dedicated virtual-currency or stablecoin statute; its money transmission law (NRS Chapter 671, modernized in 2025) does not expressly mention virtual or digital currency. Instead, the Financial Institutions Division (FID), through published guidance, applies the money-transmission licensing requirement to entities that transmit or hold digital currency, by kiosk, mobile, internet, or other means. Under that guidance, Bitcoin ATM operators must hold a money transmitter license and bond, and licensees holding virtual currency are expected to keep enough of each type to cover outstanding customer obligations. Entities acting purely as digital-asset custodians may instead be regulated as trust companies under NRS Chapter 669. Nevada thus regulates digital assets case by case under existing law rather than a bespoke framework.
states.effective: Money transmission law (NRS Ch. 671) in force; digital currency covered via FID guidance, not express statute
states.sourceText: NRS Ch. 671 (money transmission); applied to digital currency by Nevada Financial Institutions Division guidance

states.name: Maine
states.framework: Money Transmission Modernization Act (LD 2112)
states.detail: Maine enacted the Money Transmission Modernization Act (LD 2112), effective August 9, 2024, and opted to include the model act's virtual currency provisions. Businesses transmitting virtual currency are covered and must be licensed through the Bureau of Consumer Credit Protection, subject to a minimum net worth of $100,000 and a $100,000 surety bond or equivalent. A licensee that holds virtual currency for customers must maintain like-kind holdings sufficient to satisfy customer entitlements.
states.effective: In force since Aug. 9, 2024 (LD 2112); virtual-currency provisions adopted
states.sourceText: Maine Money Transmission Modernization Act (LD 2112); Bureau of Consumer Credit Protection

states.name: New Hampshire
states.framework: Money Transmission Act (RSA 399-G)
states.detail: New Hampshire requires a money transmitter license under RSA 399-G, administered by the Banking Department, but RSA 399-G:3, VII expressly exempts persons who sell or issue payment instruments or stored value solely in the form of convertible virtual currency, or who receive convertible virtual currency for transmission. The Banking Department confirms it neither licenses nor supervises businesses dealing solely in convertible virtual currency, including a stablecoin. However, money transmitters that handle traditional fiat currency, or that transmit both fiat and cryptocurrency, still must be licensed. Even transactions solely involving cryptocurrency remain subject to New Hampshire's Consumer Protection Act (RSA 358-A), so exemption from licensing is not the same as being unregulated. New Hampshire adopted the virtual-currency exemption in 2017; federal GENIUS Act and FinCEN requirements apply regardless.
states.effective: RSA 399-G:3, VII exempts persons dealing solely in convertible virtual currency from licensing (fiat-touching activity still licensed; RSA 358-A Consumer Protection Act still applies)
states.sourceText: N.H. RSA 399-G:3, VII; N.H. Banking Department position on crypto regulation and jurisdiction

states.name: Rhode Island
states.framework: Currency transmission law (MTMA provisions, H.B. 7282)
states.detail: Rhode Island regulates money transmission under its currency-transmission law, administered by the Department of Business Regulation, and adopted Money Transmission Modernization Act provisions through H.B. 7282. A licensee that has control of virtual currency for one or more persons must maintain, in its control, an amount of each type of virtual currency sufficient to satisfy the aggregate entitlements of those persons, a one-to-one like-kind custody standard.
states.effective: Currency transmission law in force; MTMA virtual-currency provisions adopted (H.B. 7282)
states.sourceText: R.I. currency transmission law (H.B. 7282); R.I. Department of Business Regulation

states.name: New Jersey
states.framework: Money Transmitters Act (N.J.S.A. 17:15C); Digital Asset Business Act (A3454, proposed)
states.detail: New Jersey requires a license under its Money Transmitters Act, administered by the Department of Banking and Insurance, and the statutory definition of 'payment instrument' is broad enough to bring virtual currency transmission within the licensing scheme. Separately, New Jersey's proposed Digital Asset and Blockchain Technology Act (A3454) would require any person engaging in 'digital asset business activity' with New Jersey residents to be licensed by the Bureau of Securities in the Division of Consumer Affairs, a dedicated crypto-licensing regime that would layer on top of money transmission if enacted.
states.effective: Money Transmitters Act in force (covers virtual currency); dedicated Digital Asset Business Act (A3454) pending
states.sourceText: N.J.S.A. 17:15C; N.J. Dept. of Banking and Insurance; A3454

states.name: Delaware
states.framework: Sale of Checks and Transmission of Money Act (5 Del. C. Ch. 23)
states.detail: Delaware requires a license under its Sale of Checks and Transmission of Money Act, administered by the Office of the State Bank Commissioner, for any business that receives money for transmission. Delaware applies this to virtual currency transmission in practice: major digital-asset exchanges hold Delaware money transmitter licenses. Surety bonding starts at $25,000 plus $5,000 per additional location, capped at $250,000.
states.effective: Sale of Checks and Transmission of Money Act in force; applied to virtual-currency transmission
states.sourceText: 5 Del. C. Ch. 23; Delaware Office of the State Bank Commissioner

states.name: Maryland
states.framework: Maryland Money Transmission Act
states.detail: Maryland regulates money transmission under the Maryland Money Transmission Act, administered by the Office of the Commissioner of Financial Regulation, and has modernized its framework to allow limited recognition of digital assets as permissible investments. Consistent with federal (FinCEN) treatment, Commissioner guidance provides that an administrator or exchanger that accepts and transmits convertible virtual currency, or buys or sells it, is a money transmitter. Applications include a detailed cybersecurity and IT-risk review.
states.effective: Money Transmission Act in force; virtual-currency exchangers treated as money transmitters (Commissioner guidance)
states.sourceText: Maryland Money Transmission Act; Office of the Commissioner of Financial Regulation

states.name: Virginia
states.framework: Money Transmission Modernization Act (HB 1942, Ch. 19.1)
states.detail: Virginia's Money Transmission Modernization Act (HB 1942, enacted in 2025 as Acts of Assembly Chapter 214) takes effect July 1, 2026, replacing Chapter 19 of Title 6.2 with a new Chapter 19.1 administered by the State Corporation Commission's Bureau of Financial Institutions. Under Sec. 6.2-1922, 'money' does not include virtual currency, and because money transmission is defined in terms of money, payment instruments, and stored value, a business engaged solely in transmitting virtual currency, including a stablecoin, generally falls outside the licensing requirement. In practice the exclusion is narrower for stablecoins: activity that also moves fiat currency, such as redemption or on- and off-ramps, transmits 'money' and can still be captured. Federal GENIUS Act and FinCEN requirements apply regardless.
states.effective: MTMA (HB 1942, Acts ch. 214) effective July 1, 2026; virtual currency expressly excluded from 'money' (Sec. 6.2-1922, Ch. 19.1)
states.sourceText: Va. Code Sec. 6.2-1922 (Ch. 19.1, eff. Jul. 1, 2026; HB 1942 / Acts ch. 214); SCC Bureau of Financial Institutions

states.name: South Carolina
states.framework: South Carolina Uniform Money Services Act (Title 35, Ch. 11)
states.detail: South Carolina regulates money transmission under the South Carolina Uniform Money Services Act (Title 35, Chapter 11), administered through the Attorney General's Money Services Division. The chapter, originally the Anti-Money Laundering Act, was renamed the Uniform Money Services Act by 2024 Act No. 218 (effective July 2, 2024). The statute defines 'money transmission' in terms of payment instruments, stored value, and receiving money for transmission, and 'monetary value' as a medium of exchange; it does not expressly address virtual currency. The Money Services Division's interpretive position is that virtual currency lacks the characteristics of a medium of exchange and does not, by itself, qualify as 'monetary value,' so virtual currency alone, including a stablecoin, is not treated as money transmission. Because this is an interpretive position rather than a statutory exclusion, it could change. The exclusion is also narrower for stablecoins in practice: activity that also transfers fiat currency, such as redemption or on- and off-ramps, may fall within the Act. Federal GENIUS Act and FinCEN requirements apply regardless.
states.effective: Uniform Money Services Act (Title 35, Ch. 11; renamed by 2024 Act No. 218) in force; Money Services Division interprets virtual currency as not 'monetary value,' so VC alone is not money transmission
states.sourceText: S.C. Code Title 35, Ch. 11 (Uniform Money Services Act); S.C. Attorney General, Money Services Division

states.name: Massachusetts
states.framework: Money Transmission Law (Ch. 312 of the Acts of 2024; MGL c. 169B)
states.detail: Massachusetts overhauled its money transmission regime with Chapter 312 of the Acts of 2024, codified as MGL c. 169B ('Regulation of Money Transmission') and administered by the Division of Banks. The Division began licensing and regulating money transmitters under c. 169B effective January 1, 2026 (NMLS applications opened around July 1, 2025). The Division has stated that most common transactional virtual currencies, expressly including stablecoins such as USDC, are a form of 'monetary value' under c. 169B, Sec. 1, so virtual-currency transmission requires a license. Non-convertible or closed-loop currencies (for example, in-game currency) are not 'monetary value.' The law is consumer-protection focused, covering money transmission for personal, family, or household purposes.
states.effective: MGL c. 169B (Ch. 312, Acts of 2024); licensing effective Jan. 1, 2026; Division treats transactional virtual currency including USDC as 'monetary value' (licensed)
states.sourceText: Mass. Ch. 312, Acts of 2024 (MGL c. 169B, Sec. 1); Massachusetts Division of Banks FAQs

states.name: Pennsylvania
states.framework: Money Transmission Business Licensing Law, as amended by Act 7 of 2025
states.detail: Pennsylvania regulates money transmission under the Money Transmission Business Licensing Law, administered by the Department of Banking and Securities (DoBS), and virtual currency is now covered by statute. After 2019 DoBS guidance that virtual currency was not 'money' and a 2024 policy statement reversing that view, the legislature enacted Act 7 of 2025, which amended the law to expressly require a license to 'engage in the business of transmitting virtual currency ... for a fee' and retitled it to reference virtual currency. Entities that transmit virtual currency, including stablecoins, for a fee generally must be licensed, subject to exemptions such as transmission solely for personal or household use and certain self-hosted wallet activity. The Act takes effect 60 days after enactment (August 26, 2025), and DoBS rescinded its prior virtual-currency statement of policy. Note that a federally-permitted payment stablecoin issuer (PPSI) is preempted by the GENIUS Act from Pennsylvania's licensing requirement for its stablecoin activity; non-issuer transmitters remain subject to it.
states.effective: Money Transmission Business Licensing Law as amended by Act 7 of 2025; effective 60 days after enactment (Aug. 26, 2025); virtual-currency transmission for a fee licensed by statute
states.sourceText: Pa. Act 7 of 2025 (Money Transmission Business Licensing Law); Pa. Dept. of Banking and Securities

states.name: District of Columbia
states.framework: Money Transmissions Act (D.C. Code Title 26, Ch. 10)
states.detail: The District of Columbia requires a money transmitter license from the Department of Insurance, Securities and Banking (DISB) under the Money Transmissions Act (D.C. Code Title 26, Chapter 10). By DISB bulletin, the District treats Bitcoin and other virtual currency used as a medium of exchange, method of payment, or store of value as money for money transmission purposes, so receiving for transmission, storing, or taking custody of virtual currency from consumers, via kiosks, mobile apps, or online, requires a license. Simply buying or selling virtual currency for cash via kiosk or online is not treated as money transmission. Bonding is $50,000, up to $250,000 across locations.
states.effective: Money Transmissions Act in force; DISB treats virtual currency as money (transmission/custody licensed; simple buy/sell carved out)
states.sourceText: D.C. Code Title 26, Ch. 10; DISB cryptocurrency & money transmission bulletin
